key_insight: Machine-learning-accelerated path-integral simulations show that nuclear quantum effects strongly enhance hydrogen diffusion through layered MoS2 and produce a clear hydrogen–deuterium isotope separation effect.
engineering_relevance: These results inform the design of 2D membrane, storage, and fuel-cell materials by quantifying how stacking, twist, and quantum nuclei alter hydrogen transport rates.
method: Interatomic potentials trained on r2SCAN+rVV10 DFT data
Well-tempered metadynamics combined with path-integral molecular dynamics
Compared multiple MoS2 polytypes and twisted bilayers with H vs D
evidence_signal: The simulations report lower hydrogen free-energy barriers at 300 K, a 35 meV H–D quantum barrier difference, and enhanced H self-diffusion relative to classical nuclei.
watch_item: The conclusions depend on the accuracy and transferability of the trained potentials and on simulation conditions that may not capture all real defect, strain, or environmental effects.
paper_title: Capturing Nuclear Quantum Effects in Hydrogen Diffusion through MoS2 via Machine-Learning-Enhanced Path-Integral Simulations
